channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - wccftech.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for wccftech.com

item.title: ‘I Don’t Love AI Slop Myself’: Jensen Huang Is “Empathetic” Towards NVIDIA DLSS 5 Critics, But Still Says That is Not What The Tech is Doing
item.content:encoded:
The controversy surrounding the reveal of NVIDIA DLSS 5 is showing no signs of cooling down. A good portion of the gaming community and plenty of developers have been vocally critical of what has been shown last week, and NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang understands where the criticism is coming from, as he told Lex Fridman in a new interview that he doesn't love "AI Slop," which sounds ironic coming from one of the key individuals spearheading the AI revolution.

"I think their perspective makes sense and I can see where they're coming from, because I don't love AI slop myself," Huang said when asked about the massive backlash after the new version of the upscaler was announced last week. "You know, all of the AI generated content increasingly looks similar and they're all beautiful. So, I'm empathetic towards what [critics] are thinking."

While agreeing to some extent to the aversion pretty much everyone has towards AI slop, Jensen Huang has reiterated again how turning games into AI slop that not only look very similar but that takes away any artistic individuality is not what NVIDIA DLSS 5 does. "I showed several examples of it, but DLSS 5 is 3D conditioned, 3D guided. It’s ground truth structure data guided. And so the artist determined the geometry, we are completely truthful to the geometry in every single frame," he said.

With NVIDIA DLSS 5 closer to a toon shader that generates visuals based on the original art style and thus respectful of the artist's intention, Jensen Huang stressed that the new version of the tech will be just another tool for developers to use.

While it is true game developers won't be forced to use it in any way, we have seen how NVIDIA DLSS Super Resolution and Frame Generation, and other similar tech from AMD and Intel, have essentially become a requirement for games. A requirement that, in some cases, is used to compensate for technical shortcomings, which is one of the reasons why many are against it in the first place.

The discussion surrounding NVIDIA DLSS 5 was essentially dominated by the now infamous Resident Evil Requiem comparison, which showed how protagonist Grace Ashcroft changed with the upcoming tech. This, and showing the tech in a way too early state, was one of the biggest mistakes NVIDIA made when making its announcement, according to our own Alessio Palumbo, who also had the chance to talk with multiple developers, such as Denis Dyack, and get their perspective about one of the most controversial new tech in gaming in a long time.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/i-dont-love-ai-slop-jensen-huang-nvidia-dlss-5/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Molyneux’s God Game Masters of Albion Opens Closed Beta Playtest Signups Ahead of April 22 Launch
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday, independent UK developer 22cans announced that signups for the Masters of Albion Closed Beta Playtest are now open through 6 PM GMT on Wednesday, March 25. The signups are handled directly through the official Discord channel. The playtest will help 22cans refine the game ahead of the planned April 22, 2026 launch on Steam.

Masters of Albion is the upcoming God game designed by Peter Molyneux, arguably the genre's creator with the classics Populous and Black & White. Molyneux, also widely known for Dungeon Keeper and Fable, said this is the culmination of his life's work in the industry.

The game is set in a medieval fantasy version of Albion, where magic is returning to the land and threatens to tear society apart. You play as the Chosen One, a god-like entity gifted with ancient powers, tasked with defending civilization against a creeping, sorcerous enemy. This God game blends elements from other genres, such as town management and tower defence. For example, Masters of Albion gives the player the chance to enter the bodies of the people and explore the world through their eyes, tackling challenges and quests, fighting bandits, searching for secrets and treasures, and roaming freely across the world.

The defining hook is a day/night duality:​

  • Daytime: You wield a "god hand" to build and customize your village from the ground up: designing homes, food production chains, clothing, weaponry, and infrastructure. The tone is described as "cozy creativity."
  • Nighttime: The world becomes dangerous as monsters and dark magical forces attack. Gameplay shifts into real-time defence, where you build walls, place turrets, hire heroes, and can even possess individual heroes or animals directly to fight in combat.

If you're interested in the Masters of Albion Closed Beta Playtest, you'll have to make sure your PC meets or exceeds the following system requirements.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/molyneux-god-game-masters-of-albion-closed-beta/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Nintendo Reportedly Cuts Switch 2 Production By More Than 30% Following Weak Holiday Sales
item.content:encoded:
• 0-20%: Unlikely - Lacks credible sources
  • 21-40%: Questionable - Some concerns remain
  • 41-60%: Plausible - Reasonable evidence
  • 61-80%: Probable - Strong evidence
  • 81-100%: Highly Likely - Multiple reliable sources

This morning, Bloomberg reports that Nintendo is cutting Switch 2 production by more than 30% for this quarter. The original plan was to make 6 million units of the new hybrid console, but Nintendo allegedly revised this down to 4 million units following weaker-than-expected Holiday sales, especially in the United States.

This reduction should not prevent Nintendo from reaching the anticipated goal of 20 million units sold by the end of the fiscal year, which ends later this month. The Nintendo Switch 2 had already sold 17.3 million units as of December 31, 2025, as reported in February, far outpacing the initial sales of the previous Switch console.

Before accelerating hardware production, Nintendo is allegedly waiting to see if demand picks up again following new software releases. One game that's promising in this regard is Pokémon Pokopia, which stunned everyone with a critical reception that turned out to be the best ever for any Pokémon game, thanks to an average Metacritic score of 90/100, and strong debut sales (2.2 million units in the first four days). Pokopia also earned strong praise from resident Nintendo expert Nathan Birch, who rated it 9 out of 10:

Pokémon Pokopia isn't just possibly the best spinoff this series has ever seen, it also captures the spirit and fulfillment of those classic early entries in the franchise better than anything that's come our way in some years. This game is mechanically sound, technically rock-solid, and has a pinch of genuine creative inspiration and joy that you don't always get from modern Pokémon titles. While the game may start a bit slow for some tastes, I think most folks who give Pokopia a chance will find they Kanto get enough.

The next big Nintendo Switch 2 exclusive, Yoshi and the Mysterious Book, is due to launch on May 21, 2026.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/nintendo-cuts-switch-2-production-weak-holiday-sales/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
